title: TinCan v1: implemented hosted MCP edge wrapper at /mcp on the existing AMS Worker as Streamable HTTP (POST = JSON-RPC, GET = SSE notifications, DELETE = end-of-session) with three tools (ams_create_conversation, ams_join, ams_send) plus ams_recv as the long-poll degradation path
content: Built worker/src/mcp.ts (~720 lines) implementing the MCP Streamable HTTP transport per the public spec (protocolVersion 2025-06-18) and an in-Worker SessionDO class for the per-(account_id, conversation_id) wrapper state. POST /mcp accepts JSON-RPC singletons (initialize, ping, notifications/initialized, tools/list, tools/call); GET /mcp opens an SSE stream for notifications/ams/{token,stream_metadata,stream_joined,stream_left,closed}; DELETE /mcp tears down the session. The four tools shipped in this slice cover the full SPEC §3.1 items 4–5 acceptance: ams_create_conversation (mints via the existing createConversation handler), ams_join (resolves the magic link, validates the permissive token, dials the upstream wire WS via env.CONVERSATION_DO directly, and hands back a self-describing mcp-session-id encoding (account_id, do_name)), ams_send (translates to a wire {type:token,data} frame; opaque), ams_recv (drains the bounded recv buffer with optional long-poll). The ams_set_metadata and ams_leave tools from canon/constraints/mcp-wrapper-conformance-for-conversational-ai are NOT shipped in this slice — wrapper-stays-cheap discipline holds the surface to what the §3.1 items 4–5, §3.2 demo gate, and charter §6 browser overlay actually require. The other two are an additive day-one extension when a consumer asks. Confidence: established (translation-only contract; no new wire). Reversibility: two-way (additive surface, no cross-client breakage from later expansion). Disconfirmer: if the wrapper translation introduces observable wire-divergent behavior (token loss, ordering inversion, capability stripping) the slice is wrong; the SessionDO holds no transformation logic on the inbound path so the disconfirmer is hard to fire by construction.

title: Adopted ams://canon/decisions/D0019 account-conversation keying for the SessionDO from day one even though TinCan v1 ships without the D0016 buffering primitive
content: worker/src/mcp.ts:sessionDoName(accountId, conversationId) is the DO's idFromName input. A reconnect from the same account into the same conversation deterministically lands on the same DO instance. The DO refuses (do_key_mismatch, 500) if a re-bind ever attempts to mutate the keying tuple — defense in depth even though idFromName makes this unreachable. The mcp-session-id is self-describing (mcps_<rand>.<b64url(account_id)>.<b64url(do_name)>) so the GET /mcp leg can route without reading any server-side index. Per charter §3 constraint 3, this is locked from day one so that future TinCan clients do not break when D0016 buffering layers on per the operator premortem (charter §5). Confidence: established (matches D0019's stated keying). Reversibility: per D0019 itself — one-way once a buffered tier ships, two-way before that. TinCan v1 is pre-buffered-tier, so the door is technically still open, but no reason to walk back. Disconfirmer: if a use case surfaces where two MCP transport sessions under one account need ISOLATED per-session buffers (rather than the cooperative-tenant model D0019 specifies), the keying choice would be wrong. None observed in this build.

title: Built browser §03 TinCan demo as a vanilla-JS MCP client speaking the same /mcp wrapper any agent uses, NOT as a wire-extension or a homepage-only path
content: worker/src/homepage.ts gains a §03 section titled "Browser as MCP runtime. Same wrapper any agent uses." The IIFE auto-mints a per-tab demo bearer (POST /v1/accounts), runs the MCP initialize handshake, calls tools/call ams_create_conversation with a stream_metadata.capabilities.ams.convention.v1 manifest declaring role=operator function=observer posture=alerting (the round-trip charter §3 constraint 1 demands), shows the magic link with a Copy button, calls tools/call ams_join to attach the browser as its own subscriber, opens the SSE leg via fetch+ReadableStream (EventSource cannot set Authorization), and renders incoming notifications/ams/{token,stream_joined,stream_left,stream_metadata,closed} into a console pane. The Emit textbox calls tools/call ams_send. CSS is ~80 lines of new .tincan-* classes; the existing .theatre/.frame primitives are reused for the SIM in §02 (which stays — the homepage-governance.md "Demo Surfaces" list keeps the SIM honest). Per ams://canon/decisions/D0012, this is the canonical path: the browser is a runtime; runtimes get wrappers. Confidence: established (D0012 settled this 2026-05-03). Reversibility: two-way (the SIM is unchanged; the live demo composes alongside it). Disconfirmer: if the live demo proves slow or unreliable in production, revert to a SIM-only homepage and revisit the wrapper's latency budget (canon/constraints/mcp-wrapper-conformance-for-conversational-ai §"Latency and Delivery Budget" recommends ≤1s median push delivery). Cannot disconfirm from this build session — sandbox has no network egress.

title: Honored charter §3 constraint 2 (token data opaque) by writing zero log/parse paths through `data` in worker/src/mcp.ts
content: grep verification on the implementation: `args.data` appears at exactly two lines in mcp.ts (line ~885 typeof guard, line ~898 forwarded into JSON.stringify({type:"token",data:args.data})). No console.log of token data anywhere. SessionDO.onWireFrame strips the wire 'type' tag and broadcasts the rest unchanged via Object.entries (no per-key transformation). The recv buffer holds {method, params} — params is whatever the wire delivered, untouched. Capabilities pass through stream_metadata in both directions because the wrapper never opens that key. Per charter §3 constraint 2 and ams://canon/constraints/wrapper-stays-cheap §"What a Wrapper Does Not Carry" (no parsing data, no schema-validating metadata, no caching, no business rules). Confidence: established (the constraint is a code-level invariant verifiable by grep). Reversibility: one-way invariant — any future code that reads token data is a constraint violation, not an additive change. Disconfirmer: if grep ever finds a console.log of token data or a content-derived branch, the constraint failed.

title: Build-session sandbox cannot reach ams.klappy.dev or ams.truthkit.ai (HTTP 403 "Host not in allowlist")
content: Confirmed by curl https://ams.klappy.dev/healthz returning 403 Host not in allowlist. This is the same constraint observed in 2026-05-03-day3-morning-build.tsv C row (Day 3 morning verification was local-only). The build session's verification surface is therefore: TypeScript typecheck (PASS, npx tsc --noEmit clean exit 0), homepage architectural-claims CI guard (PASS, scripts/check-homepage-architectural-claims.mjs reports "all architectural surfaces are N-peer compliant"), and code-level grep checks for charter §3 / §4 conformance. Live acceptance evidence (SPEC §3.1 items 1–5, §3.2, charter §6) is the fresh-session validator's responsibility against the deployed Worker per TINCAN-POC-PLAN.md §5. Captured to journal/evidence-day3-tincan-build-session.txt with the validator handoff list.

title: Wrangler [[migrations]] requires a v2 migration row to introduce SessionDO alongside the existing ConversationDO
content: worker/wrangler.toml gains a second [[migrations]] block with tag = "v2" and new_classes = ["SessionDO"]. The existing v1 migration declared ConversationDO and is preserved. Per Cloudflare's migration model, additive class introduction is an additive migration tag; renames or transfers would need different shapes. Both [[durable_objects.bindings]] entries (CONVERSATION_DO, SESSION_DO) are wired with their class names so the runtime can resolve env.SESSION_DO at request time. Confidence: established (matches Cloudflare DO migration docs). Disconfirmer: if the deploy hook fails to apply the v2 migration, the SESSION_DO binding will be undefined and ams_join will throw. The validator should observe a successful first /mcp tools/call ams_join response (status 200 with mcp-session-id header) as positive evidence the migration applied.

title: The MCP edge wrapper's ams_join is structurally cheaper than a re-walk of /v1/{ns}/conversations/{alias}/connect because the in-Worker ConversationDO route accepts a JoinPayload header directly
content: worker/src/mcp.ts:dialWire bypasses the public /connect path and forges an internal Request to env.CONVERSATION_DO.idFromName(conv_id).fetch with the X-AMS-Join-Payload base64-JSON header that the DO already accepts (worker/src/conversation.ts:handleConnect). This avoids re-doing KV lookup, permissive-token hash compare, and X-AMS-Stream-Metadata header validation that the wrapper already did at the tools/call level. The wire's structural exclusion (D0009) and conformance properties hold because the JoinPayload shape is identical — the DO sees the same payload regardless of whether it came from public /connect or wrapper-internal dialWire. The wrapper does not bend the wire; it only avoids re-validating what it already validated. This is a wrapper-stays-cheap success: no new code path, no duplicate validation, no opportunity for the two paths to drift.

title: EventSource cannot set Authorization headers, so browser MCP clients must use fetch+ReadableStream for the SSE leg of Streamable HTTP
content: The §03 TinCan IIFE in homepage.ts opens GET /mcp via fetch with an AbortController, then drains the response body through a TextDecoder + double-newline-split SSE parser. EventSource is the standard browser SSE primitive but its constructor accepts only the URL (and a withCredentials flag) — there is no headers option, so the Authorization: Bearer ams_sk_... header that the wrapper requires cannot be set. fetch+ReadableStream is the universally-supported alternative and is what real MCP clients (Claude Code's browser leg, Cursor's MCP runtime, claude.ai) all use. Documented inline in worker/src/homepage.ts startSSE() so the next browser-MCP-runtime author does not re-discover this. This corroborates D0012's "browser is a runtime" framing — the wrapper absorbs the impedance mismatch rather than the wire bending.

title: The MCP Streamable HTTP "session resumption" model is partially implemented in this slice: the SessionDO survives across MCP transport sessions per D0019 keying, but the wrapper does not yet expose the SSE Last-Event-ID resume on GET /mcp
content: The MCP spec defines an event-id-based replay path on the SSE leg for clients that disconnect and reconnect; the SessionDO does not currently buffer SSE events with stable IDs (the recv buffer is shared with ams_recv and is drained on read). Two reconnects from the same MCP client today see (a) any frames buffered between the two GETs are replayed once via the recv-buffer drain on attach, then (b) live frames forward. This satisfies the demo-gate latency budget and the SPEC §3.1 item 5 "within 5 seconds" requirement, but a long-disconnected client may miss frames that arrived and were already drained by ams_recv on a prior tenant. Buffer-shaped event-IDs are the natural shape for D0016 buffering when it lands; for now the constraint is: TinCan v1's session resumption is best-effort within a single MCP-session-id's tenancy, not the full Last-Event-ID replay model. Charter §4 "no buffering primitive" forbids the latter; this is consistent.

title: The mcp-session-id format encodes (account_id, do_name) in the suffix, which is fine for routing but means rotating an account_id requires re-issuing the session id
content: makeSessionId(accountId, doName) embeds both as base64url segments so handleMcpGet and handleMcpDelete can route without server-side state. The format is opaque to clients (they echo whatever ams_join returned) so the encoding is reversible by the wrapper alone. If a future feature rotates account credentials in-flight, sessions issued under the old credential remain valid until torn down (the credential mapping happens at the per-request authenticate() call, not at session creation), but if the account_id itself changes (which it does not under the current model), the suffix would no longer match. The constraint is benign under the current account model; flag for the post-PoC bearer-scope-management work to revisit.

title: TinCan v1 verification was strictly local (typecheck + CI guard + grep) — fresh validator MUST run §4 acceptance against the deployed Worker
content: Per TINCAN-POC-PLAN §5, the validator session reads this journal and runs SPEC §3.1 items 1–5 + §3.2 demo gate + charter §6 browser overlay against the deployed ams.klappy.dev / ams.truthkit.ai with disposition ACCEPT / ITERATE / PIVOT. Specific tests the validator should run: (1) curl POST /v1/accounts and POST /v1/{ns}/conversations on both hosts (already shipped, sanity check); (2) wscat /connect round-trip (already shipped, sanity check); (3) examples/two-agents/test-mcp-pair.mjs against the hosted /mcp by setting it as the MCP server URL (the script today drives the stdio mcp-server.mjs; a small adapter tweak points it at /mcp); (4) two MCP-speaking Claude Code instances configured per the README MCP snippet — Klappy's instance ams_create_conversation, share link, Ian's instance ams_join, ams_send, observe push notification on Klappy's; (5) a clean browser session minting on the homepage and watching two attached agents render. If the operator premortem fires (token loss between MCP transport sessions because of turn-based behavior), the disposition is PIVOT — D0016 is already-locked canon and the keying convention is in place; the build-from-pain layer is the next iteration.

title: Build session output: one PR on branch claude/tincan-day3-build-CppRn with these concern groups
content: (1) worker/ — new src/mcp.ts (the wrapper), modified src/index.ts (route /mcp, export SessionDO), src/types.ts (Env.SESSION_DO binding), wrangler.toml (DO binding + v2 migration). (2) homepage — modified worker/src/homepage.ts adding §03 TinCan section: ~95 lines new CSS, ~30 lines new HTML, ~210 lines new JS IIFE. The §02 SIM theatre is unchanged. (3) docs — modified README.md with the TinCan v1 status block + the MCP server config snippet (".mcp.json" example) + the connect-non-MCP-client subsection refactored to clarify the wire-direct vs hosted-/mcp split. (4) journal — this TSV + evidence-day3-tincan-build-session.txt. SPEC.md is NOT changed (contract envelope unchanged; the MCP wrapper was already specified in §3.1 items 4–5 and §4 "MCP edge wrapper as the agent door"; this slice ships the implementation against the existing contract). PROTOCOL.md is NOT changed (no wire change per charter §4 guardrail). Per AGENTS.md mode discipline + TINCAN-POC-PLAN §5, validation MUST happen in a fresh session; this builder cannot validate its own work. PR is marked draft until the validator's disposition lands. v0.1.0 tag is NOT cut from this build session per klappy://canon/principles/verification-requires-fresh-context — the fresh validator cuts the tag on ACCEPT disposition from the merged commit.

title: If the operator premortem fires, the PIVOT path is layer in D0016 buffering as a wrapper-layer primitive on the same branch with charter §4 amendment
content: Per TINCAN-CHARTER §5 and TINCAN-POC-PLAN §6: turn-based MCP transports (Claude Code per turn, Claude Desktop per turn) open fresh transport sessions; agent-A emitting while agent-B's MCP client is between turns means tokens vanish. If the validator observes this in §3.1 item 5 or §3.2, the disposition is PIVOT. The keying convention (D0019, already in place — see this TSV's D row 2) is forward-compatible with D0016's per-stream ring with TTL + size bounds. The buffer would attach to the SessionDO (already keyed correctly) and serve cross-MCP-session continuity for ams_recv consumers. Charter §4 guardrail "no buffering primitive built into TinCan" is amended on the same branch with explicit reference to the build evidence. This is "the plan working as designed" — build from pain, not theory.

title: No new canon articles in this slice — TinCan v1 composes from already-locked canon
content: Charter §3's "binding canon references" enumerates D0001, D0002, D0006, D0009, D0010, D0012, D0013, D0017, D0019, mcp-wrapper-conformance, wrapper-stays-cheap, observability-payload-boundary, permanent-non-goals, operator-as-subscriber, observability-as-subscriber, security-as-subscriber-pattern. All are in place; this slice is implementation against existing canon, no encode-class artifacts forced. The journal entry itself is the durable record. If the validator session surfaces a learning that justifies a new canon article (e.g., a wrapper-translation pattern that generalizes beyond MCP), that is the validator's encode call.

title: Open: ams_set_metadata and ams_leave are not shipped in this slice; degrade if a consumer requires either
content: canon/constraints/mcp-wrapper-conformance-for-conversational-ai names six tools as the conversational-AI conformance set; this slice ships four (the demo-gate-relevant subset). The two omitted tools are additive expansions: ams_set_metadata translates to a wire set_metadata frame (the wire already accepts it; SessionDO would forward); ams_leave detaches and tears down the SessionDO if the last tenant leaves (the detach handler exists in this slice as /__do__/detach and is wired to DELETE /mcp). Re-entry signal: first consumer that asks for either, or first composition pattern that requires runtime metadata mutation. Cost to add: under one hour each.

title: Open: the /mcp endpoint does not implement rate-limiting or per-IP throttling
content: Per ams://canon/decisions/D0012 "What This Means in Practice" #4, abuse-vector mitigation against /mcp belongs at the wrapper layer. This slice ships nothing: any caller with a valid bearer can mint and emit at full speed. The PoC's traffic envelope makes this acceptable in the short term; the re-entry signal is the first abuse incident or the first paying customer requirement (whichever fires first). Mitigation shape when it lands: per-IP token bucket in KV, plus per-account stream-count cap (PROTOCOL §6 close 4003 reservation already exists for the wire-side analog). Not a charter §4 guardrail — limits live in front of the wrapper, not in it.

title: Open: the homepage §03 demo's per-tab bearer is not garbage-collected
content: The browser-as-MCP-runtime IIFE auto-mints a tincan-<random> namespace + bearer per page load. Closing the tab discards the credential client-side, but the AMS account record persists in KV indefinitely. The PoC's KV size is far below any concerning bound, but at the limit this would accumulate. Re-entry signal: KV hot-storage approaching the free-tier threshold or the first observability subscriber wanting to filter demo-account telemetry from production-account telemetry. Mitigation shape: a separate "demo" namespace tier with TTL, OR a token-bucketed per-IP mint cap. Out of TinCan v1's scope.
